item.title: The song in film
item.description: Anahid Kassabian argues that a pop song in a film does something a composed orchestral score cannot: it carries the audience’s own life into the theater.

item.title: Ryuichi Sakamoto
item.description: Ryuichi Sakamoto built pop songs out of arcade-game bleeps in the late 1970s and won an Academy Award for scoring a Bertolucci epic a decade later.

item.title: Bernard Herrmann
item.description: Alfred Hitchcock wanted the shower scene in Psycho to play in silence.

item.title: Curtis Mayfield
item.description: Curtis Mayfield taught himself guitar tuned to the black keys of the piano, an open F-sharp tuning almost nobody else used, and sang above it in a high, sweet falsetto.

item.title: Modern classical
item.description: For roughly three centuries European art music agreed, in broad terms, on how music worked: major and minor keys, a home chord to leave and return to, dissonance as tension and consonance as rest.

item.title: Cinematic classical
item.description: In 1933 a Viennese émigré named Max Steiner laid continuous orchestral music under most of a film about a giant ape, and in doing so fixed the grammar of how movies would sound for the next ninety years.

item.title: Cooke — A History of Film Music
item.description: Author: Mervyn Cooke Title: A History of Film Music Year: 2008 Type: Academic survey (Cambridge University Press) When Cambridge University Press set out to publish a single-volume history of film music, it commissioned the scholar who had already written the field’s reference definition: Mervyn Coo...

item.title: (1978) Ambient 1: Music for Airports
item.description: “1/1” began as an accident Brian Eno found on playback: he had set a small group improvising exercises in a London studio, the musicians barely able to hear one another, and somewhere in the reel two pianos, unbeknownst to each other, had played melodic lines that interlocked.

item.title: Classical music
item.description: In 1829, the English organist Vincent Novello wrote in his diary of a Continental chapel that it was the place “I should come to every Sunday when I wished to hear classical music correctly and judiciously performed” — the first sentence the Oxford English Dictionary can find with the family’s name...

item.title: Dionne Warwick
item.description: Listen to the way Dionne Warwick navigates the melody of “Walk on By” — the intervals are wide, and the phrasing syncopates against Burt Bacharach’s rhythm track in ways that make the emotional content of the lyric (heartbreak, composure, the effort of pretending not to care) feel inevitable rather...
